steps.title: Pin down the goal
steps.content: The student wants to emphasize how hot the Sun is relative to nearby stars. So the answer must directly compare the Sun's temperature with that of its neighbors and show the Sun coming out on the hot side.

steps.title: Use the notes to check the comparison
steps.content: Of 357 nearby stars, 327 are K or M (cooler than the Sun) and only 11 are A or F (hotter). So the Sun is hotter than the large majority of nearby stars, but not all of them — that is the comparison the choice needs to state.

steps.title: Eliminate by rule
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the Sun is hotter than most but not all of the stars within 10 parsecs': explicitly ranks the Sun against its neighbors and lands on "hotter than most," which emphasizes how hot the Sun is relative to them. ✓
  • Choice B — 'the Sun... is a G star, and several other stars... are A or F stars': lists classifications side by side but never says the Sun is hotter or cooler than those stars, so it does not emphasize the Sun's relative heat. Misread of the goal. ✗
  • Choice C — '327 are classified as K or M stars, with surface temperatures under 8,900°F': focuses on how cool the neighbors are and never mentions the Sun's temperature, so it emphasizes the other stars, not the Sun. Misread of the goal. ✗
  • Choice D — 'most of the stars... are classified as K, M, A, or F stars, the Sun is classified as a G star': contrasts classifications but lumps hotter (A, F) and cooler (K, M) stars together, hiding the comparison and failing to emphasize how hot the Sun is. Overreach that blurs the data. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
